1: Jehoshaphat returns to his house in Jerusalem in peace (bᵉšālôm) — the very condition Ahab claimed would prove Micaiah a false prophet (2 Chronicles 18:26). Jehoshaphat’s safe return is YHWH’s mercy on the compromised king who cried out in extremity. The peace he returns to is not the peace of the righteous but the grace granted to the repentant — and it comes with a prophetic rebuke (v2).
2: Jehu son of Hanani rebukes Jehoshaphat: hᵃlārāšāʿ laʿăzōr ûmᵉśōnᵉʾê YHWH tᵉʾāhāḇ — “Should you help the wicked and love those who hate the LORD?” The two verbs — ʿāzar (help) and ʾāhēḇ (love) — name the covenant bonds that Jehoshaphat has misapplied: the ḥesed (loyal love) that belongs to fellow covenant-keepers has been extended to the LORD’s enemies. Prophetic rebuke after narrow escape is the Chronicler’s pedagogical pattern: grace is followed by instruction on why grace was needed.
3: ʾăḇāl dᵉḇārîm ṭôḇîm nimṣᵉʾû ḇāḵ — “Nevertheless, some good is found in you.” The word ʾăḇāl (“but/nevertheless/however”) is the pivot of the rebuke: the prophet acknowledges genuine goods before cataloguing failures. Jehoshaphat has destroyed the Asherahs (haʾăšērôt) and set his heart to seek God (lāḏᵉrōš ʾĕlōhîm). The Chronicler’s balanced accounting — good noted alongside failure — models the nuanced evaluation of human character that neither whitewashes nor despairs.
4: Jehoshaphat goes out among the people from Beersheba to the hill country of Ephraim and brings them back to YHWH. The king as itinerant reformer — personally traversing the land to restore covenant faithfulness — is a distinctly Chronicler theme. The geographic sweep from Bᵉʾēr-šeḇaʿ ʿaḏ-har ʾeprayim signals the pan-Israelite reach of the reform: from the deep south to what was once the northern heartland.
5: Jehoshaphat appoints judges (šōpᵉṭîm) in all the fortified cities of Judah, city by city. The judicial reform — establishing an infrastructure of legal accountability throughout the kingdom — is the institutional expression of the theological principle: YHWH’s justice must be embodied in human structures. The legal-reform initiative echoes Moses’ establishment of judges (Exodus 18:13-27; Deuteronomy 16:18-20) and anticipates the NT vision of the church as a community of justice.
6: Jehoshaphat charges the judges: rᵉʾû mah-ʾatttem ʿōśîm kî lōʾ lāʾādām tišpᵉṭû kî lYHWH — “Consider what you do, for you judge not for man but for the LORD.” The theological grounding of human justice is radical: judges do not merely serve social order; they execute YHWH’s own judgment on his behalf. Lōʾ lāʾādām... kî lYHWH (not for man but for the LORD) establishes divine accountability as the ground of judicial integrity. He will be with them in the matter of judgment.
7: wᵉʿattāh yᵉhî paḥaḏ-YHWH ʿălêḵem šimrû waʿăśû kî-ʾên ʿim-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû ʿawlāh ûmᵉśōʾ pānîm wᵉmiqqaḥ-šōḥaḏ — “The fear of the LORD be upon you. Take care what you do, for there is no injustice (ʿawlāh) with the LORD our God, no partiality (mᵉśōʾ pānîm — literally ‘lifting of faces’), no taking of bribes (miqqaḥ-šōḥaḏ).” Three negatives define divine character: no iniquity, no face-lifting (= favoritism, preferring the powerful), no bribery. The human judge is to mirror the divine judge in all three dimensions.
8: In Jerusalem Jehoshaphat appoints some Levites and priests and heads of families as a high court. The two-tier judicial system — local judges in the cities (vv5-7) and a high court in Jerusalem (vv8-11) — mirrors the Mosaic structure of Exod 18 (local judges + Moses as appeals court). The judicial infrastructure is not merely administrative but theological: the Jerusalem court handles cases that are “the LORD’s concerns” as well as those of the king.
9: Jehoshaphat charges the high court: kōh taʿăśûn bᵉyirʾat YHWH beʾemet ûḇᵉlēḇāḇ šālēm — “Thus you shall do in the fear of the LORD, in faithfulness (beʾemet), and with a whole heart (bᵉlēḇāḇ šālēm).” The three-part charge combines the dispositional (yirʾat YHWH), the epistemic (beʾemet), and the relational (lēḇāḇ šālēm) — the Chronicler’s signature phrase appearing here in the judicial context. Judicial integrity is an expression of whole-hearted covenant devotion.
10: The high court is to handle any case: bloodshed or law, commandment, statute, or rule — instructing people so they may not become guilty before YHWH. Wᵉhizkarttem ʿălêhem wᵉlōʾ yiʾšᵉmû lYHWH — “Thus you shall warn them, so that wrath may not come upon you and your brothers.” The judicial function includes a teaching dimension (hizzîr = to warn/instruct) — the court is an instrument of covenant education as much as legal adjudication.
11: Amariah the chief priest presides over all matters of the LORD, and Zebadiah the ruler of Judah presides over the king’s matters. The dual leadership — priestly and civil — reflects the Chronicler’s consistent theocratic vision where the two orders (priest and king) operate in distinct but complementary spheres. Ḥizqû waʿăśû — “Take courage and do it.” The Levites are officers to aid them in this work.
